coredata.dc:title: C. Halicacabum derived “turn-on-off” non-invasive molecularly imprinted polymer for selective profiling of 2-(3,4-dihydroxy phenyl) ethylamine in human fluid samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Murugan, Komal
Natarajan, Abirami
Rajaram, Arulmozhi
coredata.dc:description: A rationally designed “Turn on-off” molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe SCDs-MPs serves as a robust platform for the specific identification of aberrant dopamine (DA) levels. In this study, carbon dots (SCDs) were synthesized using Cardiospermum Halicacabum leaf extract as a carbon source via a step hydrothermal process. The synergistic integration of the synthesized fluorophore SCDs (QY–27.6 %) with molecularly imprinted elements via reverse micro-emulsion technique forms novel C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs nanocomposite. The synthesized SCDs-MPs possess various inimitable properties such as hydrophilicity (WCA of 12.9°) suitability for detection in aqueous media, the narrow bandgap of 3.45 eV attributed to the electron-donating nature, and photostability. Fluorescence lifetime, from 6.38 ns to 6.17 ns after adding dopamine to SCDs-MPs, and zeta potential studies (–32.9 mV to –23.9 mV) validate the static fluorescence quenching mechanism in SCDs-MPs. Under various modifications and experimental conditions, SCDs-MPs consistently achieved a low dopamine detection limit of 6.4 pM within the range of 0–50 µM. Excellent recovery experiments in human fluid samples (98–102 %) confirm the fluorescent probe’s empirical reliability. The MTT assay confirms the superior biocompatibility of SCDs-MPs, with cell viability remaining at ± 24.03 % at a concentration of 200 µg/ml suggesting the safe usage for invitro administration. This opens new possibilities for utilizing C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs material in biomedical contexts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophilic
C. Halicacabum
Fluorescence lifetime
Quenching
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of SCDs (silane functionalized carbon dots)
Synthesis of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs nanocomposites
Physiochemical characterizations of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs
Electrochemical properties of SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs
Optical properties of SCDs, SCDs-MPs, and SCDs-NPs
Fluorescence sensing mechanism of the proposed sensor
Detection of DA in human fluid samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu-603 203, India for providing the required facilities to complete the research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) SAED pattern, (c) High-resolution image of the lattice fringes, (d) EDS analysis of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs (e, f) SEM images of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs.
(a, b) XRD, FTIR spectrum of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs + DA, SCDs-MPs, and SCDs-NPs. (c) Thermal behavior studies. (d) – (i) XPS analysis C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs.

Contact angle (a, b) C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs. (c) Cyclic voltammogram of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs. (d) Absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra, and (e) Fluorescence intensity at the different excitation wavelengths of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs.

(a) Fluorescence response with different concentrations of DA in C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs, (b) Integrated SCDs-MPs emission against DA concentration (0–1000 nM): Inset- Linearity graph (F0/F and concentration of DA in nM). (c) Fluorescence lifetime studies. (d) UV–Vis spectrum of DA and Fluorescence emission spectrum of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs. (e) Fluorescence response with different concentrations of DA in C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-NPs, (f) Integrated SCDs-NPs emission against DA concentration (0–1000 nM): Inset- Linearity graph (F0/F and concentration of DA in nM).
In vitro cytotoxicity effect of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs against HeLa Cell lines: (a – f), and (g) % Cell viability Vs Concentration of SCDs-MPs in µg/mL.
Synthesis of SCDs using one-step hydrothermal method.
Synthesis of C. Halicacabum-derived SCDs-MPs via reverse microemulsion polymerization.
The formation of imprinted sites on SCDs-MPs and the interaction between the dopamine- SCDs-MPs during binding and elution process.
Detection limit of DA using C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs is compared to other detection approaches.
DA detection in human fluid samples using C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: C. Halicacabum derived “turn-on-off” non-invasive molecularly imprinted polymer for selective profiling of 2-(3,4-dihydroxy phenyl) ethylamine in human fluid samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603 203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rationally designed “Turn on-off” molecularly imprinted fluorescent probe SCDs-MPs serves as a robust platform for the specific identification of aberrant dopamine (DA) levels. In this study, carbon dots (SCDs) were synthesized using Cardiospermum Halicacabum leaf extract as a carbon source via a step hydrothermal process. The synergistic integration of the synthesized fluorophore SCDs (QY–27.6 %) with molecularly imprinted elements via reverse micro-emulsion technique forms novel C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs nanocomposite. The synthesized SCDs-MPs possess various inimitable properties such as hydrophilicity (WCA of 12.9°) suitability for detection in aqueous media, the narrow bandgap of 3.45 eV attributed to the electron-donating nature, and photostability. Fluorescence lifetime, from 6.38 ns to 6.17 ns after adding dopamine to SCDs-MPs, and zeta potential studies (–32.9 mV to –23.9 mV) validate the static fluorescence quenching mechanism in SCDs-MPs. Under various modifications and experimental conditions, SCDs-MPs consistently achieved a low dopamine detection limit of 6.4 pM within the range of 0–50 µM. Excellent recovery experiments in human fluid samples (98–102 %) confirm the fluorescent probe’s empirical reliability. The MTT assay confirms the superior biocompatibility of SCDs-MPs, with cell viability remaining at ± 24.03 % at a concentration of 200 µg/ml suggesting the safe usage for invitro administration. This opens new possibilities for utilizing C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs material in biomedical contexts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The catecholamine neurotransmitter Dopamine [2-(3,4-Dihydroxyphenyl) ethylamine] is a good-feel hormone that plays important roles in the neurological, renal, and cardiovascular systems. Dopamine regulates hormone release, kidney blood flow, circulation maintenance, and more. Dopamine concentrations in humans range from 0.1 μM (blood and urine) to 0.5 to 25 nM (cerebrospinal fluid). The aberrant change in Dopamine concentration causes Parkinson's, Huntington's, Schizophrenia, memory loss, and epilepsy [1]. To detect central nervous system illnesses, neurochemical dopamine in biological fluids must be identified.

Different analytical methods have been used to develop diverse dopamine sensing systems, including photo-electrochemistry, Raman spectroscopy, chemiluminescence, chromatography, electrochemical sensing, capillary electrophoresis, and immunoassays [2]. However, these methods have limitations such as complex sample preparation and high cost. Fluorometric sensing methodologies are considered ideal due to their high sensitivity, selectivity, non-destructive nature, and cost efficiency [3]. However, dopamine detection in a complex biological media with great sensitivity and selectivity is necessary.

Carbon dots (CDs) are increasingly used in fluorometric sensing analyses due to their optical properties. CDs are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes under 10 nm [4,5]. The conjugated system has sp2 hybridization and carbon sheets ranging from disordered graphite to amorphous carbon. CDs have good photostability, bright photoluminescence, tunable surface functionalities, long lifetime, high quantum yields, ease of synthesis, low background noise, and low toxicity [6]. Green chemistry is increasingly interested in natural carbon nanodots, which offer several benefits [7,8]. This study uses a new carbon precursor, Cardiospermum halicacabum leaf extract (Balloon vine), a Sapindaceae plant. Traditional remedies for diarrhoea, haemorrhoids, asthma, muscular pains, pyodermas, carbuncles, earaches, rheumatism, ophthalmias, and arthritis include balloon vine leaves [9]. Cardiospermum halicacabum contains steroids, triterpenoids, flavonoids, stilbene, Vitamin C derivatives, etc. C. halicacabum leaf extracts have anti-arthritic effects due to their bioactive components. Lack of selectivity and active sites are the key challenges of the CD-based fluorescence probe. A molecularly imprinted method improves carbon dot sensor detection. This approach is commonly used to identify sensor system units [10].

The molecular imprinting technique is an effective strategy for producing polymeric artificial receptors for specific molecular recognition [11,12]. The method of preparing molecularly imprinted polymers (MPs) was derived from the concept of antibody and antigen-specific recognition in biological immunology [13]. The MPs binding efficiency, recognition processes, and selectivity have been intensively studied by optical techniques. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MPs) have been widely used in different fields, such as optical and electrochemical sensors, drug delivery, catalysis, and solid-phase extraction [14,15]. The MPs that have been introduced enhance the absorption behavior with great selectivity, and the addition of CDs helps to transduce the chemical interactions into detectable FL signals. They are also adaptable, stable, cost-effective, and simple to prepare. Thus, molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors play a crucial role in the field of molecular recognition by combining the merits of the high sensitivity of CDs and the high selectivity of MPs [16].

In this work, we report a fluorescent probe for the detection of dopamine (DA) based novel C. Halicacabum derived molecularly imprinted SCDs-MPs nanocomposite. Here C. Halicacabum leaf extract is chosen as a carbon source, and APTES for silane functionalization was first organized for the preparation of silane-functionalized carbon dots (SCDs) by one-step hydrothermal method. The C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs were synthesized using the reverse microemulsion method by using SCDs, template molecules (DA), functional monomers (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane), crosslinkers (tetraethoxysilane), and an ammonia solution was added as the catalyst. The fluorescence intensity response and the application ability of the newly synthesized sensor were thoroughly assessed through a range of experiments, and the potential mechanism was examined and discussed.

The chemicals and solvents were of laboratory grade and utilized without further refinement. Dopamine hydrochloride, n-hexane, Triton X-100, Cyclohexane, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Vijaya Scientifics supplied an aqueous ammonia solution, methanol, acetone, acetic acid, acetonitrile, and ethanol. Glucose, fructose, ascorbic acid, citric acid, uric acid, epinephrine, and norepinephrine were received from SISCON, India. Sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, copper sulfate, potassium nitrate calcium chloride, ferric nitrate, and aluminium sulfate De-ionized water was acquired from a MilliQ purification system and utilized in all experiments in this research work.

Hi-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (JEOL Japan, JEM-2100 Plus) was utilized for contemplating average diameter and morphology. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) by FEI Quanta FEG200 for surface structure. X-Ray Diffractometer (XRD) BRUKER USA D8 Advance, Davinci to test the nature. For the study of optical properties Photoluminescence spectrometer (Flurolog HORIBA) and Uv–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, UV 3600 PLUS) scan range of 200–800 nm were utilized. FTIR spectrometer by SHIMADZU, IRTRACER 100 analyses the functional groups. The chemical compositions were predicted via X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) PHI Versaprobe III. Contact Angle Meter (HOLMARC Opto-Mechatronics) was used to measure the water contact angle. Thermo Gravimetric Analyzer, Netzsch-STA 2500 Regulus was utilized to determine the thermal stability (TGA). Cyclic voltammetry was performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660C) with three electrodes: a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), a counter electrode (Pt), and a glassy carbon working electrode. The measurements were taken from 1.0 V to −1.0 V, with a scan rate of 50 mV/s−1. Fluorescent lifetime analysis was performed on an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer. Zeta potential was measured on a Malvern Zeta sizer instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SCDs (silane functionalized carbon dots)
ce:section.ce:para: The leaves of Cardiospermum Halicacabum (English name: Balloon vine) which belongs to the family Sapindaceae were collected from the Kancheepuram district, Tamil Nadu, India, and confirmed by Dr. M. Ganesan, Asst. Prof [S.G], IIISM, SRMIST. Silane-functionalized carbon dots from Cardiospermum Halicacabum leaf extract were inferred through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Initially, 50 gm of Cardiospermum Halicacabum of leaf extract was washed a few times with water to remove the impurities and made into juice with 100 ml of deionized water. Then 55 ml of the extract was mixed with 20 mL of APTES and degassed with nitrogen for around 20 min under continuous stirring where Cardiospermum Halicacabum acts as a carbon source and APTES is used for silane functionalization. Then, the autoclave was tightly closed and maintained at 210 °C for 5 h in order to carbonize and functionalize the surface. Following this, the solution's color changed from green to dark brown, and the crude product was centrifuged. The SCDs were gathered and kept in storage at 4 °C for later usage. The synthesis of SCDs using a one-step hydrothermal method is shown in Scheme 1 .

The quantum yield for the synthesized SCDs was calculated using the equation
Q X
=
Q Y
×
A Y
I Y
×
I X
A X
×
η X 2
η Y 2
where
Q X
,
Q Y
is the quantum yield for SCDs (sample − X) and quinine sulphate (reference − Y),
I X
,
I Y
stands for emission intensities,
η X 2
,
η Y 2
denotes the refractive index and
A X
,
A Y
is the absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: By using a modified reverse microemulsion technique, novel C. Halicacabum derived molecularly imprinted SCDs-MPs nanocomposite were synthesized [17]. 18 mL of cyclohexane as a continuous phase, 3.8 mL of co-surfactant n-hexanol, and 4 mL of surfactant Triton X-100 were added successively into the RB flask and stirred for 25 min to form a microemulsion. Followed by the addition of 5 mL of SCDs with 10 min of stirring, and then 280 μL of TEOS and 100 μL of NH3H2O were added, respectively, and stirred for 2 h. 1.2 mL of n-hexanol was combined with 20.6 mg DA and 260 μL APTES before being progressively added dropwise to the solution. For 24 h in the dark, the solution was mechanically agitated. After demulsifying the mixture with 20 mL of acetone, particles were obtained by centrifuging the mixture at 5000 rpm. To remove DA and contaminants, the precipitate was cleaned with ethanol and acetonitrile combined at a 9:1 ratio (Fig. S1). SCDs-NPs underwent the same preparation procedure as C. Halicacabum-derived SCDs-MPs but without the addition of the template as represented in Scheme 2 .

The intensity of fluorescence was measured under the same conditions throughout the experiment with parameters such as excitation wavelength 310 nm (the selected spectral range 300–700 nm), 10 nm slit widths, and a 600 V excitation voltage. In a 4 ml quartz cuvette, 400 µg/mL−1 of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs was mixed with PBS buffer (pH = 7.0), varying concentrations of DA, and then distilled water was added to reach the volume of 3.6 mL. FL measurements were recorded after a 10-minute incubation period. The same process (except DA) was used to assess the C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-NPs as well as the SCDs-MPs fluorescence intensities. Before usage, each solution was shaken adequately to ensure thorough dispersion.

The experiment of selectivity: 100 µL of six structural analogs, namely dopamine, glucose, fructose, ascorbic acid, uric acid, epinephrine, and norepinephrine, were added to 400 µg/mL−1 of the C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs solution. The fluorescence intensities of every single mixture were measured and documented. The same adsorption experiment was carried out for SCDs-NPs to investigate the differences in the adsorption characteristics of SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs. The anti-interfering ability of the proposed sensor was also tested with the following metal ions: Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ for both SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs. For each mixture, the fluorescence intensities were measured and recorded.

The viability and accuracy of the synthesized C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs fluorescent sensor are tested in human fluid samples (serum and urine) obtained from the Life Science Laboratory, SRMIST. Before the analysis, the human blood serum samples were diluted 500-fold. Human urine samples were treated with 10 ml of acetonitrile to remove the macroparticles and precipitate proteins. Next, the sample is centrifuged at a speed of 8000 rpm for 15 min, causing the supernatant to separate. This supernatant is then carefully filtered using a membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µM. Afterward, the resulting sample is diluted with ultrapure distilled water at a ratio of 50 parts water to 1 part sample. The diluted blood serum and urine samples were spiked with various concentrations of DA under optimal conditions according to the DA detection procedure.

Cell viability in the presence of SCDs-MPs was studied using the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay [18]. To perform the MTT assay, the HeLa cell lines derived from human cervical cancer cells were placed in a 96-well culture plate alongside various concentrations of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs (ranging from 0 to 200 µg/mL-1). The plate was then incubated at 37 °C and humidified with a 5 % CO2 incubator. Following a 48-hour incubation period, MTT was added to the wells and left for 3 h at room temperature. The content in all the wells was then removed using a pipette, and 100 µl of SDS in DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. Finally, the absorbances were measured at 540 nm using a Lark LIPR-9608 microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical characterizations of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and dimensions of the C. Halicacabum derived SCDs and SCDs-MPs were examined using HRTEM. The finding of SCDs was discussed in the supporting information (Fig. S2). The results indicate that SCDs-MPs have an average particle size of 12.9 nm. Fig. 1 a, depicting the TEM image of nanocomposite SCDs-MPs, indicates that the particles are aggregated and the SCDs are covered by a thin polymer matrix. Additionally, the diffused ring patterns in SAED verify that the synthesized SCDs-MPs are amorphous in nature (Fig. 1b). High-resolution images of lattice fringes present in SCDs-MPs with a lattice spacing of 0.49 nm are illustrated in Fig. 1c. The particle size of the polymers was apparently greater than that of SCDs (3.5 nm) Fig. S2b, implying that the polymers SCDs-MPs have been produced, and the rough surface shows the prevalence of several cavities in the polymer matrix. Moreover, the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis revealed the chemical compositions C (22.14 %), N (10.09 %), O (41.98 %), and Si (25.78 %) in the SCDs-MPs shown in Fig. 1d.

Fig. 1e and f display the SEM images of as-prepared SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs. The SCDs-MPs are mostly spherical in structure with a rough surface, but there is some aggregation. Fig. S3(a–d) presented the elemental mapping outcomes of SCDs-MPs. The corresponding findings revealed that the elements C, N, O, and Si were uniformly distributed, indicating the homogeneous nature of the SCDs-MPs produced using the reverse microemulsion method. These results unequivocally demonstrated the successful formation of SCDs-MPs.

The XRD patterns reveal the degree of crystallinity or amorphous nature, and physical properties. The broad peak at 2θ = 21.9° is ascribed to the (002) plane of carbon material (Fig. S4a) [19]. Fig. 2 a depicts the XRD patterns of SCDs-MPs, SCDs-NPs, and SCDs-MPs + DA measured in the range from 10° to 80°. The existence of wide and low peaks at 22.6°(220) appeared due to the encapsulation of silane-functionalized carbon dots surface onto the molecularly imprinted polymer as well as the non-imprinted polymer, and it also specifies that the carbon atoms are erratically arranged. The peak at 26.5° indicates the characteristic peak of DA imprinted with SCDs-MPs. Nevertheless, the specific peaks seen in SCDs-MPs + DA were lacking in the SCDs-MPs as well as SCDs-NPs spectrum, demonstrating that the template molecule DA has been removed from the imprinted layer [23]. This demonstrates that the selective imprinted sites are low in SCDs-NPs. These results clearly show the amorphous nature of the synthesized probe.

FT-IR spectroscopy was implemented for SCDs, SCDs-MPs, SCDs-MPs + DA, and SCDs-NPs to determine the functional groups present on the surface. Fig. S4b depicts the FT-IR spectra of SCDs, representing the characteristic peak at 3334 cm−1 due to –OH stretching. The N-H bending and stretching vibrations are responsible for the peaks around 3200 cm−1. The Si-O-CH stretching vibration emerges at 1036 cm−1 caused by APTES bonding on the surface of the C-dots. The existence of –CH2- and C = O was shown by the peaks at 2940 and 1565 cm−1, respectively [20]. The FT-IR spectra of SCDs-MPs + DA, SCDs-MPs, and SCDs-NPs are shown in Fig. 2b. The peak around 2940–2864 cm−1 is related to the –CH stretching vibrations [21]. The absorption peaks at 785 cm−1 correspond to Si-O vibrations, 696 cm−1 indicates the presence of Si-O-C vibrations, and the peak at 1051 cm−1 shows the existence of Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching vibrations. Moreover, the characteristic peaks for the existence of dopamine (SCDs-MPs + DA) appear at 3256 cm−1, 1619 cm−1, and 1529 cm−1 corresponding to the phenol-OH stretching vibrations, amine N-H bending vibrations, and aromatic CC stretching vibrations [22]. These peaks were absent in the FT-IR spectra of SCDs-MPs, suggesting that the template dopamine has been eluted to form the imprinted cavities. The intensity and position of the characteristic peaks were significantly similar for C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs.

TGA investigation under nitrogen flow examined the thermal behavior of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs, SCDs-MPs, and SCDs-NPs (Fig. 2c). SCDs TGA curve showed an initial weight loss of 10.56 % from 25 °C to 150 °C, equivalent to their absorbed water content. Weight loss owing to decomposition was 45.94 % between 150 °C and 500 °C. SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs TGA curves showed that water molecules released at 98 °C caused initial weight loss. Ethanol (from template molecule elution) caused a second weight loss at 240 °C. SCDS-MPs and SCDs-NPs lost 49.3 % and 42.38 % of their weight during thermal degradation at 250 °C to 600 °C. After heating to 800 °C, SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs did not lose weight. Compared to the SCDs-TGA curve, SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs had better thermal stability. Their improved thermal stability implies they might be used in high-temperature applications.

XPS was used to analyze the elements present on the surface of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs. Fig. 2(d, e) illustrates the elemental composition (%) and whole SCDs-MPs survey spectrum. The four distinct peaks at 284.09 eV, 401.5 eV, 533.1 eV, and 102.8 eV are ascribed to the C1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Si 2p, respectively. Fig. 2f shows the C1s high-resolution spectra, which indicate that 284.8 eV refers to the C-N bond and the CO bond appears at 287.46 eV. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) is deconvoluted into two peaks, one at 399.4 eV and the other at 400.6 eV, owing to the C-N-C and N-H, respectively [21,23]. From Fig. 2h, the deconvoluted O 1s spectra, it is evident that the peak that occurred at 532.4 eV is assigned to the C–O bond and 531.3 eV corresponds to the CO bonds. The absorption peaks at 102.8 eV and 101.7 eV correspond to Si–C and Si–O bonds in the deconvoluted Si 2p spectra (Fig. 2i). These findings from XPS analysis match the acquired FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b) as well.

The contact angle of the material specifies the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of the material. It also stipulates the suitability of the as-proposed material for detection in the aqueous medium. Using the drop contour analysis, the water contact angle measurements were taken, and the shadow image of the drop was optically evaluated. The surface is wettable when the angle is between 0° and 90°. We refer to this surface as hydrophilic. Conversely, if the angle is above 90°, the surface is non-wettable. In this case, we refer to it as hydrophobic [24]. The contact angles of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs were 12.9° and 13.4°, respectively (Fig. 3 a, b). This indicates that the contact angle of the synthesized C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs has a good hydrophilic character, and the as-proposed sensor is suitable for the detection of DA in the aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical properties of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs were determined to assess the effect of silane-functionalized carbon dots incorporated into molecularly imprinted polymer on electrochemical behavior. Fig. 3c shows the cyclic voltammetric data of the SCDs-MPs recorded at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 in a solution containing ferrocyanide-ferricyanide (Fe (CN)6 3-/4-) as an electrolyte. Based on the CV data, the oxidation onset potential (Eox) is determined to be 0.046 eV. By analyzing the absorbance spectrum, the band gap (Egap) is found to be 3.34 eV. The half-wave potential (E1/2) of ferrocene was found to be 0.16 eV. Using the empirical relation,
EHOMO = − [(Eox − E1/2 ) + 4.8 eV]
the calculations for the HOMO energy level of SCDs-MPs were calculated [25,26]. The EHOMO was found to be −4.68 eV and the band gap value of 3.34 eV led to the determination of the ELUMO energy level at −1.34 eV.

The cyclic voltammogram of SCDs-NPs was depicted in Fig. 3c. The Eox was found to be 0.06 eV, and from the absorbance spectrum, the Egap was measured to be 3.45 eV. The E1/2 was taken to be 0.16 eV. Using the empirical relation mentioned above, the HOMO energy level of SCDs-NPs was calculated to be −4.72 eV. The ELUMO was found to be −1.27 eV, corresponding to the EHOMO and Egap values of the SCDs-NPs. In derived SCDs-MPs, the presence of recognition cavities and electron-donating groups on the polymer matrix increases the energy of the HOMO level and leads to a narrower band gap compared to SCDs-NPs. Therefore, the CV results confirm the SCDs-MPs possession, the energy shift in the band gap, and the change in the electrochemical behavior. These factors may impact the interaction of SCDs-NPs with other biological molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of SCDs, SCDs-MPs, and SCDs-NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Using UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy, the optical properties of the SCDs are investigated. The absorbance peak at 274 nm visible in Fig. S5a indicates that aromatic sp2 domains of the carbon core are in a π-π* transition [27]. The CO and other functional groups that are found on the surface of SCDs are attributed to the shoulder peak at around 316 nm. The emission and excitation spectra of SCDs are shown in Fig. S5b. The emission wavelength of 435 nm shows the highest fluorescence intensity, with an excitation wavelength of 316 nm. By using quinine sulfate as a reference, it was discovered that the QY (quantum yield) of the synthesized SCDs was 27.6 %.

SCDs-MPs were excitedbetween 300–340 nm, with the highest emission intensity at 435 nm at 316 nm (Fig. 3d, e). Fig. S5c shows that following template extraction, SCDs-MPs (B) virtually achieved SCDs-NPs (A) fluorescence intensity. SCDs-MPs' fluorescence intensity decreased considerably while interacting with DA molecules (D), suggesting a charge transfer between SCDs and DA molecules. This recognition system used SCDs and DA molecules as electron donors and acceptors. The addition of the template molecule DA increases the MPs layer-SCD electrostatic interaction sites, attracting more SCDs and neutralizingmore charges. Thus, SCDs-MPs photoluminescence is suppressed by DA molecules. Thus, SiO2 nanomaterials were used to synthesizeSCDs-MPs to improve sensitivity and inter-layer interaction.

The optimization conditions have a significant impact on the C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs sensor fluorescence response. The fluorescence intensity and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of SCDs-MPs are greatly impacted by the pH of the solution, the effect of dispersing solvents, temperature, the concentration of SCDs-MPs, the incubation period was discussed in Supplementary material (Figs. S6 and S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing mechanism of the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs have specific imprinted sites corresponding to dopamine molecule configurations. Subsequently, the molecularly imprinted polymer layer possesses a greater affinity for the dopamine molecule. In this process, SCDs were used as optical antennas for distinguishing DA molecules associated with a molecularly imprinted polymer matrix. The fluorescence emission intensity of SCDs-MPs was quenched by the addition of dopamine molecules, resulting in a lower fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4 a). Due to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface, the SCDs-MPs behave as negatively charged species, and with –NH3 +, the dopamine molecule behaves as a positively charged species. Additionally, SCDs-MPs zeta potential before and after DA addition was measured. SCDs-MPs' zeta potential decreases dramatically from –32.5 mV to –23.9 mV after addition (Fig. S8). Thus, the charged SCDs-MPs enable a non-covalent interaction with the diols and amine functional groups in the dopamine molecule (positively charged) through electrostatic interactions, π–π stacking, and hydrogen bonding under neutral conditions. The dopamine molecule is oxidized to dopamine-quinone, which accepts the electron from the SCDs-MPs, which results in the fluorescence quenching process [28]. This fluorescence quenching effect is due to the interruption of the electron-hole pair of SCDs by the dopamine molecule in the imprinted sites at the molecularly imprinted polymer matrix, resulting in a lower fluorescence emission intensity as depicted in Scheme. 3 .

The fluorescence lifetime experiment was carried out for SCDs-MPs, SCDs-MPs + DA, and SCDs-NPs (Fig. 4c). The average lifetime of SCDs-NPs and SCDs-MPs is 6.88 ns and 6.38 ns, respectively. After the addition of the DA molecule to the SCDs-MPs, the lifetime was changed to 6.17 ns. The insignificant change between 6.38 ns and 6.17 ns demonstrates that the quenching may be a static quenching process. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) method is excluded because it is clear from Fig. 4d that the emission spectra of the SCDs-MPs and the absorption spectra of the DA do not overlap. This phenomenon suggests that the DA may effectively shield the excitation light for SCDs-MPs, which is an impressive feature of the static quenching effect. The sensitivity of the designed fluorescent probe SCDs-MPs is improved by the application of high-fluorescent SCDs [10]. The Stern-Volmer equation was used to correlate the relationship between fluorescence intensity and DA concentration.
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
[ D A ]
where F0, F indicates the fluorescence intensity of SCDs-MPs without DA and with varied addition of DA. Fig. 4b depicts the significant linearity between the fluorescence intensity and various concentrations of DA, resembling a well-fitted equation F0/F = 1.00129 + 0.00619 [CDA] (R2 = 0.9986) over a linear concentration range of 0–1000 nM. To assess the sensitivity of the proposed analytical method, the Limit of Detection (LOD) is a vital parameter corresponding to the low analyte concentration and minimum level of signal detected [29,30]. The limit of detection for SCDs-MPs is 6.4 pM. This verifies that the fabricated fluorescence sensor can be a promising method for the trace quantification of DA. Fig. S9 clearly shows the quenching process of SCDs-MPs in the presence of DA. To explore the adsorption capacities of SCDs-MPs, static adsorption experiments were conducted with the addition of a DA standard solution. The absorbance of the solution was determined using a UV/Vis spectrophotometer, and the adsorption capacity was calculated. The adsorption capacity (Qe, mg/g) can be determined using the equation:
Q e
=
( C
0
-
C e
) V / m
where C 0 (mgL−1) and C e (mgL−1) are the initial andequilibrium concentrationof DA, respectively. V (L) is the solution volume and m (g) is the mass of the adsorbent of SCDs-MPs [31,32]. These experiments reveal that the SCDs-MPs have an adsorption capacity of 11.9 mg/g for DA, achieved within a saturation time of 12 mins (Fig. S10). This efficient adsorption is likely attributed to the formation of specific imprinted cavities within the SCDs-MPs. The SCDs-MPs are prepared with the addition of DA via reverse microemulsion polymerization, after which the DA is eluted from the SCDs-MPs using the solvent elution method. Thus the specific imprinted cavities for DA are formed in the SCDs-MPs. Upon rebinding the DA to the SCDs-MPs, the DA molecule becomes adsorbed to the specific imprinted sites in the SCDs-MPs, thereby quenching the fluorescence intensity of the SCDs-MPs. Additionally, hydrogen bonding and non-covalent interactions between DA and the monomer (APTES) may contribute to the adsorption mechanism. Overall, these findings demonstrate the strong affinity and rapid response of the SCDs-MPs toward DA.

The fluorescence responses of the SCDs-NPs were conducted as a control experiment. Fig. 4e shows that adding different amounts of DA has a small effect on the fluorescence intensity of SCDs-NPs. The linear equation of SCDs-NPs was found to be F0/F = 1.00649 + 0.00322 [CDA] (R2 = 0.9888) with a LOD of 1.77 nM (Fig. 4f) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) of 17.73 nM. These inferences show that the quenching effect of SCDs-MPs is higher than that of SCDs-NPs with a lower LOD value. This indicates that SCDs-MPs are more sensitive to DA than SCDs-NPs. The slope of the calibration curve represents the KSV (quenching constant) value, which estimates the sensitivity of the fluorescent assay. The KSV values of SCDs-MPs and SCDs-NPs were found to be 0.00619 and 0.00322, respectively.

Selective DA binding was performed on the proposedfluorescent probe. Interfering analytes such asglucose, fructose, ascorbic acid, epinephrine, norepinephrine, citric acid, uric acid, and metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, and Fe3+) were tested under the same circumstances. In comparison with another analyte (Fig. S11a, S11b), the DA shows the highest quenching efficiency when treated with SCDs-MPs. The molecular imprinting approach imprinted DA sites on the fluorescent probedemonstrating the excellent selectivity of SCDs-MPs. The same experiment was performed with SCD-NPs, which lack selectivity for all analytes, suggesting no imprinted sites on non-imprinted layers. These findings demonstrate that the proposed fluorescence assay C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs has great selectivity for DA and can accurately identify DA in human samples.

Moreover, the reproducibility of SCDs-MPs was evaluated by conducting a successive removal-rebinding cycle test to determine their F0/F values (Fig. S11c). The results of four repeated experiments demonstrated that the detection performance remained consistent with the initial test, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.69 %. This highlights the exceptional reproducibility of SCDs-MPs. The established fluorescence sensing approach based on SCDs-MPs is compared to other previously developed detection methods (Table 1 ). The fluorescent approach developed in this study demonstrated a wide dynamic range and a detection limit notedly lower than other studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human fluid samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs biosensors was investigated in human blood serum and urine samples. The recovery study of the analytes in real samples is also one of the criteria for evaluating the biosensor's strong performance, and the standard addition method was used to detect DA in human fluid samples. The diluted samples (500-fold blood serum and 50-fold urine samples) were spiked with DA. The experiment was carried out and measured under optimized conditions. The obtained results are represented in Table 2 . The recovery range is between 98–102 %. This indicates that the reliability and accuracy of the proposed SCDs-MPs biosensors satisfy the need for detecting DA human (blood & urine) samples.

The cytotoxicity of C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs was measured by the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) method against HeLa cell lines. For the MTT assay, different concentrations of SCDs-MPs (0.0–200.0 µg/ml) were incubated with cells. The in vitro cytotoxicity activities observed in HeLa cell lines were less affected by the different concentrations of SCDs-MPs (Fig. 5 ). In the HeLa cell line, high cytotoxicity effects were observed in the SCDs-MPs at higher concentrations at 48 h of treatment; also revealed that increased concentrations of SCDs-MPs showed increased cytotoxicity over the HeLa cell line. The IC50 concentration of the given sample of SCDs-MPs was 104.16 µg/ml against the HeLa cell line. The study demonstrates that the prepared C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs were scarcely toxic to the cells.

In this study, we developed a sensitive, highly selective, and robust fluorescent platform for detecting dopamine (DA) using C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs (fluorescent silane-functionalized carbon dots and molecularly imprinted polymer). The incorporation of SCDs into the MIP layer offered several advantages: 1) Their high affinity and specificity for DA improved the interaction between target DA and the imprinted cavity. 2) SCDs, acting as both the fluorescence signal molecule and a carrier, facilitated the binding of DA to the SCDs. 3) The synergistic binding effect of MIP and the unique luminescence properties of SCDs enhanced target interaction and reduced fluorescence intensity, resulting in high sensitivity for DA detection. We successfully detected DA in human fluid samples with recoveries ranging from 98 % to 102 % using the developed C. Halicacabum derived SCDs-MPs fluorescent probe. Additionally, the cell compatibility and non-toxic properties of the SCDs-MPs fluorescent probe demonstrate its potential for detecting DA in living organisms.

Komal Murugan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Abirami Natarajan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Arulmozhi Rajaram: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu-603 203, India for providing the required facilities to complete the research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110862.
